title: Controlling multiparameter quantum estimation in exciton-optomechanics system
authors: Hamza Harraf, Mohamed Amazioug, Rachid Ahl Laamara
abstract: Multiparameter quantum estimation has emerged as a central task in quantum metrology. In this work, we investigate multiparameter quantum estimation in a hybrid exciton--optomechanical (EOM) system. The system consists of a semiconductor quantum well embedded inside a driven optomechanical microcavity, where the excitonic, optical, and mechanical modes interact coherently through exciton--photon and radiation-pressure couplings. Using the Gaussian-state formalism, we derive the covariance matrix of the steady-state quantum fluctuations and employ both the symmetric logarithmic derivative (SLD) and right logarithmic derivative (RLD) approaches to evaluate the quantum Fisher information matrix associated with the simultaneous estimation of the exciton--photon coupling strength $g$ and the excitonic decay rate $k_x$. We analyze the corresponding quantum Cramér--Rao bounds and determine the most informative precision limit governing the attainable estimation accuracy. The influence of several experimentally relevant parameters, including temperature, driving power, optomechanical coupling strength, and dissipation rates, is investigated in detail. Our results show that strong hybrid interactions and low-temperature regimes significantly enhance the estimation precision, whereas thermal fluctuations and dissipation processes deteriorate the metrological performance. Furthermore, we compare the ultimate quantum limits with experimentally feasible Gaussian measurement strategies based on homodyne and heterodyne detection. We show that heterodyne detection provides better estimation performance than homodyne schemes and can approach the optimal quantum precision limit in suitable parameter regimes.
summary: The paper analyzes multiparameter quantum estimation in an exciton-optomechanical system to determine the ultimate limits on parameter sensing accuracy. Using advanced quantum information techniques, it shows that low temperatures and strong coupling enhance precision, while heterodyne detection offers the best practical measurement strategy compared to homodyne methods.
main_problem: The study investigates how to achieve optimal precision when simultaneously estimating multiple parameters (like coupling strength and decay rate) in a complex quantum system.
main_result: Strong hybrid interactions and low temperatures significantly boost estimation accuracy, while thermal noise degrades performance; heterodyne detection proves superior to homodyne measurement techniques.
method: The analysis employs the Gaussian-state formalism, deriving covariance matrices and utilizing both Symmetric Logarithmic Derivative (SLD) and Right Logarithmic Derivative (RLD) approaches to calculate Quantum Fisher Information Matrices.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a hybrid exciton–optomechanical (EOM) system involving semiconductor quantum wells coupled to an optical microcavity and mechanical resonator. The dynamics are modeled using bosonic operators interacting via coupling terms ($g$ and $G_0$).
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM), Quantum Cramér–Rao Bounds (QCRB), covariance matrix of quantum fluctuations, and the ratio between SLD and RLD bounds.
important_parameters: Exciton-photon coupling strength ($g$), excitonic decay rate ($\kappa_x$), temperature ($T$), driving power ($\epsilon$), and optomechanical coupling strength ($G_0$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to Gaussian quantum fluctuations, and the comparison between measurement protocols assumes ideal detection efficiency.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of the estimation precision (BMI) on temperature, driving amplitude, and decay rates; they also compare the time evolution of QFI derived from SLD vs. Homodyne/Heterodyne measurements.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by setting up the EOM Hamiltonian, linearizing the quantum Langevin equations for fluctuations to find the covariance matrix, applying advanced estimation theory (SLD/RLD) to derive bounds, and finally analyzing how environmental parameters ($T$, $\epsilon$, $k_x$) affect these ultimate precision limits.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it combines cavity QED concepts (exciton-photon coupling) with mechanical oscillators in a quantum metrology context. The detailed comparison of estimation bounds and measurement schemes provides concrete guidelines for experimental realization in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Sketch of exciton–optomechanical (EOM). A quantum well (QW) is embedded in a semiconductor microcavity formed by two distributed Bragg reflectors (DBRs), enabling interaction between the exciton mode (x) and the optical cavity mode (a). The DBRs are movable, and their mechanical motion (d) couples to the cavity photons through a dispersive interaction. The shaded grey region illustrates the mechanical degree of freedom and is not intended to represent the actual physical motion. (b) The cavity mode (a) couples to the exciton mode (x) and via a linear beam-splitter interaction (g). In addition, the cavity mode interact with the mechanical mode (d) through a dispersive...

FIG. 2. (a) Plot of the BS /BR as function of the temperature T for different values of the coupling G0. (b) Plot of the BMI as function of the temperature T for different values of the coupling G0. The other parameters are provided in the table I.
FIG. 3. Plot of the most informative bound (BMI) as function of the driving amplitude ε for different values of the coupling g. The other parameters are provided in the table I.

Figure 3 shows the dependence of the BMI on the driving amplitude ε for different values of the exciton-photon coupling strength g. One clearly observes that the BMI rapidly decreases as the driving amplitude increases and eventually approaches a nearly vanishing value. Physically, increasing the driving strength enhances the intracavity photon population, which effectively amplifies the hybrid interactions within the system. As a result, the encoded information associated with the estimated parameters becomes more efficiently transferred into the quantum fluctuations of the output state. Consequently, the distinguishability between neighboring quantum states is significantly enhanced,...
FIG. 4. Plot of the most informative bound (BMI) as function of the exciton decay rate kx for different values of the mechanical damping rate kd. The other parameters are provided in the table I.
